summary: DHH describes his new ability to blog using EspressoBlog, a native OS X application that communicates with his Movable Type blog via XML-RPC. The app solves a specific frustration with Movable Type's lack of quick formatting tags in the Chimera browser. However, he notes Espresso's limitations: no editing, no template changes, no notifications. It's a brief, practical post about tooling workflow from the very early days of blogging.
key_points: XML-RPC enables native desktop apps to interact with web-based blogging platforms
Browser compatibility issues with Movable Type drove the need for a native client
Early blogging tools were fragmented — no single app handled the full workflow
The post reflects the era's tension between native apps and web apps
money_quotes: By the powers of XML-RPC, I'm now able to update Loud Thinking from the comfort of a OS X application while still retaining the key benefits of being served by a web-application.
key_insight: Even in 2003, DHH was navigating the native-vs-web app tradeoff, using XML-RPC as a bridge to get the best of both worlds for his blogging workflow.
